lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16: Vision and Language — Multi-Modal Foundation Models

unit.name: Zero-shot CLIP classification with text prompts
unit.description: Shows how CLIP can classify new images without training a new classifier by using text embeddings as class prototypes.
unit.summary: The lecturer contrasts language models, which can use next-token prediction as a universal interface for new tasks, with CLIP, which lacks an autocomplete mechanism and otherwise needs a trained linear layer for downstream adaptation [ts=600s] [ts=634s]. The zero-shot trick is to use CLIP’s text encoder to create vector representations for every candidate class label, embed a new image with the image encoder, and classify by nearest-neighbor similarity to those text vectors [ts=660s] [ts=695s]. This is described as a one-nearest-neighbor algorithm where text embeddings serve as class centers [ts=737s]. Because CLIP was trained on phrases rather than isolated words, prompts such as “a photo of a dog” can improve ImageNet performance over bare labels [ts=760s] [ts=786s]. Prompt ensembling further improves robustness by averaging vectors across multiple templates such as “a photo of” and “a drawing of” each class [ts=808s] [ts=824s].

key_points.text: Language models can often adapt zero-shot because tasks can be cast as autocomplete, while CLIP does not naturally have that interface.
Zero-shot CLIP classification uses the text encoder to create class vectors without retraining the model.
A new image is embedded with the image encoder and assigned to the nearest text class vector.
The procedure is equivalent to a one-nearest-neighbor classifier with text embeddings as class prototypes.
Prompting with phrases like “a photo of a [category]” performs better than isolated class words.
Averaging multiple prompt vectors per class reduces template bias and yields stronger class representations.

kp_catalog.name: Zero-shot CLIP classification as nearest neighbor over text prototypes
kp_catalog.description: CLIP can classify new images by embedding candidate class labels with the text encoder and choosing the most similar text embedding.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because zero-shot CLIP classification as nearest neighbor over text prototypes formalizes how cLIP can classify new images by embedding candidate class labels with the text encoder and choosing the most similar text embedding, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Prompt templates and prompt ensembling for CLIP
kp_catalog.description: Using phrases and averaging multiple prompt embeddings improves class representations compared with bare class names.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because prompt templates and prompt ensembling for CLIP clarifies using phrases and averaging multiple prompt embeddings improves class representations compared with bare class names, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
